data.title: Sagen MI Canada reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Sagen MI Canada press release (MIC.PR.A:CA): Q1 Net income of $118 million was $13 million lower than the same quarter in the prior year

  • The Company also announced today that its Board of Directors had declared a dividend of $0.3375 per Class A preferred share, Series 1, payable on June 30, 2026, to holders of record at the close of business on June 15, 2026.

data.title: Nasdaq crosses 25K for first time as tech stocks power gains
data.description:
The Nasdaq Composite crossed the 25K mark for the first time on Friday, underscoring the index’s rapid ascent over the past decade. The milestone comes just a year after the index stood at 18K and compares with 14K five years ago and 4.8K a decade ago.

Data showed the latest 1,000-point move to 25K took 184 days, reflecting a steadier pace compared with the sharp bursts seen in 2025, when multiple milestones were achieved in under two months.

The longer-term trajectory highlighted how gains have compounded, with earlier jumps, such as from 6K to 7K, taking more than 6K days.

This is the data posted by Charlie Bilello on X: 

The latest advance was supported by broad-based strength in technology and growth stocks, with several large-cap and software names posting solid gains on the day. Momentum across semiconductors, the internet, and software segments contributed to the push higher.

Top Nasdaq 100 movers of the day:

  • Strategy (MSTR) – 6.91%
  • Intel (INTC) – 5.47%
  • Seagate Technology (STX) – 4.79%
  • Apple (AAPL) – 4.48%
  • Datadog (DDOG) – 4.39%
  • DoorDash (DASH) – 4.17%
  • Charter Communications (CHTR) – 4.14%
  • Shopify (SHOP) – 3.91%
  • Linde (LIN) – 3.20%
  • Palantir Technologies (PLTR) – 3.06%

data.title: Spirit Airlines prepares to shut down as rescue deal falters - WSJ
data.description:
Spirit Airlines (FLYYQ) is preparing to cease operations after failing to receive a $500 million lifeline from the US government. Shares of Spirit plunged as much as 74%.

The discount airline wasn't able to get enough support between certain bondholders and the government to get the financing to save the carrier, according to a WSJ report on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter. 

There was no timetable listed for when operations may cease.

Frontier Airlines (ULCC) and JetBlue (JBLU) could benefit from a competitive standpoint if Spirit (FLYYQ) completely winds down. Shares of Frontier jumped 15% on Friday, while JetBlue rose 12%.

data.title: Market breadth narrows as S&P 500 rally leans on fewer stocks
data.description:
The S&P 500’s (SP500) (SPY) (VOO) rally is masking a troubling trend beneath the surface, with fewer stocks driving the index’s gains.

According to Turning Point Market Research in an X post published Friday, the share of S&P 500 (SPY) stocks outperforming the index over a rolling 21-day period dropped to 21% on Wednesday. 

The firm noted that since 1928, there have been only 59 other instances of weaker participation.

Narrow markets can persist, particularly when megacap (MAGS) (MGC) (MGK) winners continue pulling the benchmark higher on strong earnings. However, the average stock is lagging, leaving the broader market exposed to potential weakness.

data.title: Universal Display rises after eyeing stronger 2nd half despite lowering FY outlook
data.description:
Shares of Universal Display (OLED) surged about 9% on Friday after the company said second-quarter revenue would be sequentially higher and the second half of the year would be stronger than the first half.

However, Universal lowered its full-year revenue guidance, and the company's first-quarter results missed analysts' estimates.

The company lowered its full-year revenue outlook range to $630M to $670M (midpoint at $650M) from its prior forecast range of $650M to $700M. The consensus revenue estimate is $664.70M.

Universal's CFO Brian Millard said on the earnings call that, "the operating environment has become more challenging over the past few months. Near-term visibility has declined as macro pressures weigh on consumer demand assumptions, while higher memory pricing and supply constraints continue to temper end market expectations. Based on current forecast, we expect second quarter revenue to be sequentially higher than the first quarter, and we continue to expect the second half of the year to be stronger than the first half. At the same time, given reduced near-term visibility and the evolving macro backdrop, we believe it is prudent to revise our full year revenue guidance range to $630 million to $670 million from our prior guidance range of $650 million to $700 million."

In addition, the company said that on April 28, its board authorized management to buy back up to an additional $400M in common stock. 

Universal noted that it bought back 632,673 common shares for $66.4M during the three months ended March 31. During this period, and inclusive of such purchases, the company completed its share repurchase program authorized in April 2025. Universal repurchased a total of 923,883 shares worth about $100M.

Oppenheimer kept its Outperform rating but lowered the price target on Universal's stock to $130 from $160.

"UDC navigates a more challenging 1H26 with reduced visibility, mid/low-end smartphone exposure to memory cost pressure, and an extended blue timeline. Our long-term thesis is intact: earnings growth will accelerate into 2027 supported by G8 capacity ramping and new IT products using OLED. UDC's asset-light model, IP moat, and aggressive capital return ($400M buyback) provide downside support," said analyst Martin Yang.

Roth Capital maintained its Buy rating but reduced the price target to $168 from $180.

"Unsurprisingly, OLED reported 1Q26 results below consensus given near-term softness in consumer end markets, particularly China-based smartphones which face memory headwinds. Additionally, OLED revised its 2026 outlook to $630-670M (flat y/y and vs. the prior $650-670M). However, this implies seq growth in 2Q26 and 2H26, as demand patterns appear to have stabilized and 2 8.6 gen fabs commercialize (Samsung in 2Q and BoE in mid-2026). With IT becoming a more prominent driver and Blue on the horizon, we maintain our Buy with a $168PT (vs. $180 prior)," said analyst Scott Searle.

The analysts noted that the first quarter missed on China and one-time non-cash charges.

"While 2026 features more muted market (sq meter) growth of ~2% (down from 1Q26’s ~4% sq meter growth and the fall’s 8% pre-smartphone slowdown), we believe OLED continues to track towards double-digit market growth in 2027 driven by new OLED fab capacity targeted at IT end markets with ~5% market penetration. This could be further augmented by Blue emitters which maintain an indeterminate commercial timeline despite meeting technical specs," said Searle.

data.title: Emergent outlines $170M-$185M Q2 revenue outlook while maintaining $720M-$760M 2026 revenue guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Emergent BioSolutions (EBS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We reported first quarter revenue of $156 million... Adjusted EBITDA came in at $36 million... representing a 23% margin" (CEO, President & Director Joseph Papa).

  • "In April, we announced the refinancing of our prior term loan, which enabled us to secure a more favorable interest rate" and "we also continued our share repurchase program, buying back $9 million in shares in the first quarter" (CEO Papa).

  • "Overall, MCM performed very well" and "our international markets... now represent 37% of our total MCM revenue"; on naloxone, "we continue to maintain the share leadership" and "recently launched... the NARCAN Nasal Spray carrying case and a multipack configuration" (CEO Papa).

  • "Our new strategic partnership with Substipharm Biologics enables us to restart the manufacturing of the Canton facility to support the Japanese encephalitis vaccine" and "just yesterday, we announced a second strategic manufacturing partnership with SAB Biotherapeutics" (CEO Papa).

  • "We started fiscal year 2026 with a strong first quarter with revenue exceeding the top end of our guidance" (Executive VP & CFO Richard Lindahl).

Outlook

  • "We are maintaining our full year total revenue guidance of $720 million to $760 million" (CFO Lindahl).

  • "We are updating our adjusted EBITDA guidance... and we, therefore, expect full year adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $155 million to $175 million" (CFO Lindahl).

  • "For the second quarter, we expect total revenue to be between $170 million and $185 million" (CFO Lindahl).

  • "Commercial revenues are expected to be flat to slightly up with volume offsetting anticipated price adjustments, and we expect NARCAN to maintain its leading market share" and "MCM revenues are consistent with prior guidance of flat to slightly down with a significant contribution from international sales" (CFO Lindahl).

Financial Results

  • "Total revenue for the first quarter of 2026 was $156 million" and "adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EBITDA margin were $36 million and 23%, respectively" (CFO Lindahl).

  • "Adjusted gross margin was 52%" and "operating expenses were $57 million... down $10 million year-over-year"; "R&D spend declined by about 1/3 compared to the first quarter of 2025" (CFO Lindahl).

  • "Net working capital improved by over $100 million since Q1 2025" and "we improved our cash balance by $11 million versus the prior year to $160 million, and our total liquidity increased to $260 million" (CEO Papa).

  • "We reduced net debt by $122 million or approximately 22% versus the first quarter of 2025" and "our net leverage ratio... was 2.4x adjusted EBITDA at 1Q '26 versus 2.7x at the first quarter of '25" (CFO Lindahl).

  • "$50.4 million of contingent consideration could be owed to Ridgeback Bio in the second quarter of this year" and "we have reported that amount as an accrued acquisition obligation under current liabilities" (CFO Lindahl).

Q&A

  • Jessica Fye, JPMorgan Chase & Co: Asked for a longer-term view of the naloxone franchise beyond 2026. CEO Papa: "We do think the market is going to continue to grow" and "we expect to hold on to the leading market position... [and] stay competitive on pricing," adding "Q2 is the fiscal year-end for about 70% of the states" as a seasonality factor.

  • Jessica Fye, JPMorgan Chase & Co: Asked about international MCM margin vs. U.S. MCM. CEO Papa: "We offer a most favored nation pricing type of arrangement to the U.S. government" and "our prices for other countries around the world will be slightly higher." CFO Lindahl: "You should assume that the international sales are above the average for the MCM segment in total."

  • Raghuram Selvaraju, H.C. Wainwright & Co: Asked whether the SAB partnership implies a type 1 diabetes strategic shift, plus geopolitical drivers of international MCM demand, plus whether manufacturing is rightsized. CEO Papa: On SAB, "it was really more of a technology than it was a therapeutic area approach"; on demand, "it is a more dangerous world that we live in" and "the risk of bioterror is maybe as risky, if not worse"; on footprint, "we've streamlined our footprint" and "expanding the footprint and bringing Canton back online... is an absolutely worthwhile endeavor."

  • Rishi Parekh, JPMorgan: Asked about quantifying international opportunity backlog and related margin potential. CEO Papa: "It's a little bit harder to answer the backlog question" but "there's no doubt there's incremental interest" and "anything that we sell outside the U.S., by definition, is going to have a higher price and therefore, a higher margin."

  • Alex Kelsey, Wells Fargo: Asked what the accrued acquisition obligation relates to and whether it is a 2026 cash outflow. CFO Lindahl: "That relates to the Ebanga program" and "that's going to be a cash outflow in the second quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative to neutral (analysts): Questions centered on durability of the naloxone franchise, margin structure, international backlog visibility, and cash obligations, including "is that a cash outflow that we should expect in 2026?" (Alex Kelsey, Wells Fargo).

  • Slightly positive (management): Management emphasized execution and balance-sheet actions, including "execution of our 2026 turnaround plan is well underway" (CFO Lindahl) and "we believe we have made significant headway" (CEO Papa).

  • Versus prior quarter: The current call carried more probing analyst follow-ups than Q4 2025, where "I am showing no questions at this time" (Operator) and management’s tone leaned more declarative in prepared remarks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance framework: Q1 2026 reiterated "total revenue guidance of $720 million to $760 million" (CFO Lindahl), consistent with the initial 2026 range presented in Q4 2025.

  • Profitability guidance language: Q4 2025 guided "adjusted EBITDA... between $135 million to $155 million" (CFO Lindahl, Q4 2025), while Q1 2026 stated "adjusted EBITDA... $155 million to $175 million" and noted, "beginning in 2026, we are adding back non-cash stock compensation to our adjusted EBITDA" (CFO Lindahl, Q1 2026).

  • Capital structure actions: Q1 2026 highlighted refinancing and added facilities, including "amended our revolver to $50 million" and "a new delayed draw term loan facility for $75 million" (CEO Papa), versus Q4 2025 emphasis on 2025 deleveraging and a new "$50 million authorization" for repurchases (CFO Lindahl, Q4 2025).

  • International MCM emphasis: Q4 2025 stated international sales were "34% of our full year medical countermeasures revenue" (CEO Papa, Q4 2025); Q1 2026 highlighted international markets at "37% of our total MCM revenue" in the quarter (CEO Papa).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Adjusted gross margin is expected to be between 45% and 47%, reflecting product mix and expected pricing dynamics" (CFO Lindahl).

  • "Commercial revenues are expected to be flat to slightly up with volume offsetting anticipated price adjustments" (CFO Lindahl).

  • "$50.4 million of contingent consideration could be owed to Ridgeback Bio in the second quarter" and "that's going to be a cash outflow in the second quarter" (CFO Lindahl).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as an early-year execution checkpoint with $156 million in revenue and $36 million in adjusted EBITDA, alongside refinancing steps intended to "lower[] interest costs" and extend maturities. The company maintained full-year revenue guidance of $720 million to $760 million and provided Q2 revenue guidance of $170 million to $185 million, while emphasizing international MCM expansion, NARCAN line extensions, and new partnerships that include restarting activity at the Canton facility, alongside an expected Q2 cash outflow tied to the Ebanga/Ridgeback contingent consideration.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: TC Energy posts Q1 beat, approves $1.5B Columbia Gas expansion project
data.description:
TC Energy (TRP) down 0.9% in Friday's trading after beating Q1 earnings expectations, driven by a strong performance in its North ‌American operations, and approving its US$1.5B Columbia Gas expansion project to tap strong demand in the U.S.

The Appalachia Supply Project, which is supported by a 20-year take-or-pay contract backed by a major utility, is designed to provide up to 800M cf/day of capacity for natural gas-fired power generation and has an expected in-service date in 2030.

The project is capable of providing as much as 2 Bcf/day of gas day through future expansions, which could meet expected demand for electrification, economic development, and data centers in the U.S. heartland market, the company said.

The approval came after TC Energy (TRP) in February launched a non-binding expansion project open season seeking commitments from customers for its Crossroads Pipeline system for up to 1.5 Bcf/day of capacity that was 2.5x oversubscribed; the system will serve markets in Northern Indiana, Illinois, Iowa and South Dakota.

For Q1, TC Energy (TRP) reported net income attributable to shareholders fell to C$899M (US$662M), or C$0.86/share, from C$978M, or C$0.94/share, in the year-earlier quarter, but comparable EBITDA rose 14% Y/Y to C$3.09B in the quarter.

TC Energy (TRP) said Q1 adjusted core profit from U.S. natural ​gas pipelines, its largest unit, rose ~10% to nearly ​C$1.5B, while earnings from its Canadian natural gas pipelines segment added ~3% ‌to ⁠C$919M, and adjusted core profit from its Mexico natural gas pipelines jumped 85% to C$432M.

Q1 Canadian natural gas pipeline deliveries rose 3% to 29.7 Bcf/day, and U.S. pipeline flows increased 5% to 32.6 Bcf/day, while deliveries to liquefied natural gas facilities jumped 12% ​to 3.9 Bcf/day.

data.title: Select Medical outlines $5.6B-$5.8B 2026 revenue outlook while planning 275 additional beds through 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Select Medical (SEM) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “On March 2, we announced that Select Medical entered into an agreement to be acquired by a consortium led by our Executive Chairman, Robert Ortenzio, together with Martin Jackson and Welsh, Carson, Anderson & Stowe,” said (Chief Executive Officer Thomas Mullin). “Under the terms of the agreement, unaffiliated shareholders will receive $16.50 per share in cash,” and management said it “expect[s] it to close in mid-2026,” noting the Hart-Scott-Rodino waiting period “expired on April 27.”

  • “In connection with and contingent upon the completion of the transaction, our senior secured credit facilities will provide for an additional $1 billion of term loan borrowings, bearing interest at a rate equal to SOFR plus 3%,” said (CEO Mullin).

  • “So far this year, we’ve added 166 beds across 3 newly opened inpatient rehabilitation hospitals,” said (CEO Mullin), and added, “Across the remainder of 2026 and into 2027, we expect to add 275 more beds, 209 will be in IRF and 66 in critical illness.”

  • “All 3 of our operating divisions delivered revenue growth versus the prior year period, with total revenue increasing by 5% overall,” said (CEO Mullin). “Adjusted EBITDA declined 6.5% to $141.6 million,” and “earnings per common share was $0.35,” while “adjusted for the take-private transaction costs,” EPS “was $0.36.”

  • “At the end of the quarter, we had $1.9 billion of total debt outstanding and $25.7 million of cash on the balance sheet,” said (Executive VP & CFO Michael Malatesta). “We are maintaining our full year 2026 guidance.”

Outlook

  • “We are maintaining our full year 2026 guidance,” said (CFO Malatesta). “We continue to expect revenue to range between $5.6 billion and $5.8 billion and adjusted EBITDA between $520 million and $540 million,” and “fully diluted earnings per common share is expected to be in the range of $1.22 to $1.32.”

  • Management’s guidance language was unchanged from the prior quarter’s outlook issuance for 2026, when the company also guided to revenue of $5.6 billion to $5.8 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $520 million to $540 million, and fully diluted EPS of $1.22 to $1.32.

Financial results

  • The company reported consolidated “adjusted EBITDA declined 6.5% to $141.6 million,” said (CEO Mullin), alongside total revenue growth of 5% and EPS of $0.35 (or $0.36 excluding take-private costs).

  • In inpatient rehabilitation, “revenue increased more than 14% year-over-year to approximately $351.9 million,” and “adjusted EBITDA increased 15% to $81.1 million,” said (CEO Mullin), adding that “average daily census grew 12%” and “same-store occupancy increased to 87% from 83%.”

  • In critical illness recovery hospitals, “adjusted EBITDA declined 15% to $73.4 million,” and management attributed pressure in part to Medicare Advantage dynamics discussed in Q&A.

  • In outpatient rehabilitation, “adjusted EBITDA was $22 million,” and management tied performance to operational actions and market exits discussed in Q&A.

Q&A

  • Benjamin Hendrix, RBC Capital Markets: asked about outpatient rehab margin improvement and sustainability; CEO Mullin: “We’ve been doing a lot around scheduling and schedule optimization,” and “if we do not see a path out, we’re going to be exiting those markets,” adding, “that was approximately $1 million of cost that flowed through in the first quarter… Oregon, where we closed 4 clinics.”

  • Benjamin Hendrix, RBC Capital Markets: asked for an update on broader policy efforts around the LTACH high-cost outlier; CEO Mullin: “we are encouraged” the proposal keeps the threshold consistent and said preliminary data had the company “running at or below that threshold… of 7.975% of Medicare revenue being in the outlier bucket,” while outlining a future advocacy focus to “pivot to more of the patients” excluded by prior LTAC criteria.

  • Ann Hynes, Mizuho: asked whether the company is seeing increased denials, including from Medicare Advantage; CEO Mullin: “We did see an increase… a decrease in conversion for Medicare Advantage,” adding, “we’re seeing more denials in the Medicare Advantage space for our hospitals,” while “in outpatient, it’s been relatively flat.”

  • Ann Hynes, Mizuho: asked whether anything in the inpatient rehab proposed rule was surprising; CEO Mullin: “there were no concerns with the rule… it was pretty benign and nothing out of the ordinary.”

  • Joaquin Eduardo Martinez (for Joanna Gajuk), BofA Securities: asked about CIRH gross margins and whether management expects recovery; CFO Malatesta: Medicare Advantage conversion declines “impacted our volume,” with “approximately $13 million to $14 million” year-over-year impact, and added, “we do expect to still be within our expectations for the remainder of the year.”

  • Joaquin Eduardo Martinez (for Joanna Gajuk), BofA Securities: asked for early read on the Medicare TEAM Model; CFO Malatesta: “we haven’t really seen an impact to our census in the inpatient rehab space,” and CEO Mullin: “Everything that we’ve seen so far is a very minor issue in our rehab hospitals.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to neutral, concentrating on reimbursement risk and friction points (denials and high-cost outlier) and profitability (outpatient margin, CIRH margins), including “increase in… denials” (Mizuho) and “gross margins in the CIRH segment” (BofA).

  • Management tone was neutral to slightly positive, emphasizing development momentum and stability in guidance; management used reassurance framing such as “we are maintaining our full year 2026 guidance” (CFO Malatesta) and “there were no concerns with the rule” (CEO Mullin).

  • Versus the prior quarter, the call shifted from process uncertainty (“nonbinding indication of interest” under review) to transaction specificity (cash price, timing expectations, HSR expiration) and more pointed Q&A on Medicare Advantage denials and CIRH pressure.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The biggest update was governance/strategy: Q4 described a “nonbinding indication of interest” under special committee review, while Q1 detailed a signed take-private agreement at $16.50 per share, an expected mid-2026 close, and incremental $1 billion term loan capacity contingent on completion.

  • Operational emphasis remained consistent on inpatient rehab expansion, with Q1 updating bed openings and a refined forward pipeline (“expect to add 275 more beds” across 2026-2027) compared with Q4’s broader “expect to add 399 beds” across 2026-2027.

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4’s heavy attention on outpatient margin drivers (health insurance expense, variable discounts, payer mix) to Q1’s focus on outpatient operational fixes and exits, plus Medicare Advantage denials and CIRH volume/margin sensitivity.

Risks and concerns

  • Management said Medicare Advantage pressure was evident: “We’re seeing more denials in the Medicare Advantage space for our hospitals,” said (CEO Mullin), and CFO Malatesta quantified a year-over-year impact in critical illness of “approximately $13 million to $14 million.”

  • The company highlighted execution risk in outpatient footprint optimization: CEO Mullin said, “if we do not see a path out, we’re going to be exiting those markets,” and noted clinic closures in Oregon.

  • Regulatory reimbursement remains a key swing factor, with management pointing to proposed CMS rate updates for FY 2027 and monitoring high-cost outlier mechanics; CEO Mullin said the company is “encouraged” by the proposed stability and expects to engage in “lobbying efforts and the conversations with CMS.”

Final takeaway

Management centered Q1 on the signed take-private deal (including a $16.50 per share cash price, an expected mid-2026 close, and contingent $1 billion of additional term-loan capacity) alongside continued inpatient rehabilitation expansion and unchanged full-year 2026 guidance. In Q&A, analysts pressed on outpatient margins, Medicare Advantage denials, and critical-illness profitability, with management pointing to scheduling productivity work, planned market exits where performance does not improve, and reported Medicare Advantage conversion declines as a driver of critical-illness pressure, while characterizing the TEAM model impact as limited so far.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
